ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general taco class (FOODON_00002473); no specific vegetarian taco subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Tacos — composite dish with tortilla, vegetables and legumes; vegetarian variant
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP covers composite taco-type dishes. No dedicated vegetarian taco code exists; closest parent match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for vegetarian tacos as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of vegetarian tacos is 2 tacos, weighing approximately 200 g, providing around 242 kcal with moderate carbohydrates and good dietary fibre.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetarian Tacos — Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F06010006 is sourced directly from HPB Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g macros are taken directly from the HPB record. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for vegetable taco (NDB 21340) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is documented as an adapted field.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Bean-Based and Vegetable-Filled Tortilla Dishes — analogous estimate based on corn tortilla with beans (GI ~46) from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for vegetarian tacos in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 46 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: corn tortilla with bean filling, which is classified as low GI (~46) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (46) × available carbohydrate per serving (9.4 g/100g × 200 g serving = 18.8 g) / 100 = 8.65, rounded to 8.7. This is a low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 204 mg/100g is borderline; potassium estimated at 220 mg/100g partially offsets sodium load
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 1–2 tacos and avoid adding salted cheese, sour cream, or extra salt; using lime juice and fresh herbs as flavour enhancers helps maintain a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio consistent with DASH diet principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (~46), moderate carbohydrate at 9.4 g/100g, and dietary fibre at 2.95 g/100g support glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Vegetarian tacos have a low glycaemic index and moderate carbohydrate content modulated by bean-derived fibre, making them suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when served in a 2-taco portion using corn tortillas and avoiding high-sugar toppings such as sweetened salsa or sour cream.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~1.5 g/100g), plant-based protein from beans, and dietary fibre at 2.95 g/100g support cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat content, absence of trans fat, and bean-derived soluble fibre make vegetarian tacos a heart-friendly option; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose corn tortillas over fried shells and avoid adding full-fat cheese or sour cream to keep saturated fat intake within recommended limits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high dietary fibre from beans, and anti-inflammatory plant compounds from vegetables and lime
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Vegetarian tacos are generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to their low saturated fat and high fibre content; however, nightshade ingredients including tomato, chilli, and pepper may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals and should be monitored.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetarian tacos are a plant-based Mexican-origin dish consisting of soft or crispy tortillas — made from corn or wheat flour — filled with a combination of seasoned vegetables, legumes (typically black beans or pinto beans), shredded lettuce, diced tomatoes, and cheese. The dish is assembled rather than cooked as a single unit: the tortilla may be lightly warmed, pan-fried, or baked, while the filling ingredients are seasoned separately with spices such as cumin, chilli powder, and garlic. The flavour profile is savoury and mildly spiced, with textural contrast between the crisp or soft tortilla shell and the tender vegetable and bean filling. Nutritionally, vegetarian tacos provide 121 kcal per 100 g, with moderate fat at 7.46 g (predominantly from cheese and any added oil), low-to-moderate carbohydrate at 9.4 g, and useful dietary fibre at 2.95 g per 100 g from the bean and vegetable content. Protein is modest at 2.62 g per 100 g. Sodium is borderline at 204 mg per 100 g, which warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 46 (low), making this dish suitable for glycaemic management when portion-controlled. The dish is vegetarian and halal-compatible, though not vegan due to the presence of cheese. It is widely consumed as a lunch or dinner option and is increasingly popular in Singapore and Southeast Asia as a plant-based alternative to meat-filled tacos.

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Foodo database (cr_id F06010006, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Saturated fat adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy closest equivalent. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database using corn tortilla with bean filling. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for vegetarian tacos is not reported in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for this composite dish as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in HPB source. calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data for this composite dish not available in HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy for this specific preparation as of 2026. saturated_fat_g of 1.5 g/100g is adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 21340, taco with vegetables) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown. potassium_mg of 220 mg/100g is an estimate consistent with the HPB input record narrative and USDA analogue. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: confirmed negligible for this plant-based dish with no hydrogenated fats in the standard preparation. This is a non-Singaporean dish; multilingual naming requirements for Singaporean dishes (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) do not apply. The dish is Mexican/Tex-Mex in origin and no mandatory local-language names are required under Project Veritas v2 multilingual rules for this cuisine category.
